findings.reachability: Requires an admin-level user (or second-order DB compromise) to influence the siteurl/plugins_url value stored in wp_options. The output is only rendered on the plugin settings page, accessible to users with edit_theme_options capability.
findings.justification: The inline script is built by string-concatenating admin_url() and plugins_url() directly into double-quoted JavaScript string literals without esc_js() or wp_json_encode(); if the stored siteurl contains a double-quote or backslash (possible via admin manipulation or DB tampering), an attacker can break out of the JS string context and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the admin panel.

detection_pattern.description: Detects wp_add_inline_script calls that concatenate PHP function return values directly into the script string. Files matching grep_pattern but not containing wp_json_encode or esc_js near the call site are candidates for JS-context XSS, especially when URL or user-controlled values are embedded in quoted JS string literals.

findings.reachability: Admin-only page; admin_url() returns a known-good URL in standard WordPress installs so exploitation requires unusual site configuration or a filter hook modifying admin_url output.
findings.justification: The help sidebar HTML at lines 193-198 concatenates admin_url() directly into href="..." attributes without wrapping in esc_url(); while admin_url() is generally safe, the WordPress coding standards require esc_url() on every URL echoed into an HTML attribute to defend against filter-injected malicious URLs.

detection_pattern.section_name: Missing esc_url on admin_url in href attribute
detection_pattern.description: Detects href attributes that embed admin_url() output without esc_url() wrapping. While low risk in default installs, any WordPress filter modifying admin_url output could inject a javascript: or data: URI; the fix is always esc_url(admin_url(...)).
